expectations.text: behavioral_check: Explains that rows enter PositionOutput via insert into a part table (PositionOutput.TrodesPosV1 or PositionOutput.DLCPosV1), not via populate()
expectations.evidence: Section 'How rows actually get in': 'You populate the upstream Computed table normally — e.g. TrodesPosV1.populate(key) or DLCPosV1.populate(key)... The upstream pipeline... inserts a corresponding row into PositionOutput and the matching part table — typically through PositionOutput.insert1(...) or the helper PositionOutput().merge_populate("TrodesPosV1")'.

expectations.text: behavioral_check: Names at least one valid downstream consumer pattern (e.g., merge_get_part(key).fetch1('KEY'))
expectations.evidence: Canonical fetch pattern shown: `merge_key = PositionOutput.merge_get_part(key).fetch1("KEY"); df = (PositionOutput & merge_key).fetch1_dataframe()`.

expectations.text: behavioral_check: Does not recommend PositionOutput.populate() or populate(PositionOutput, ...) as the entry point — explicitly contrasts with how rows actually arrive (insert into a part table)
expectations.evidence: Opens with 'merge masters don't have one in the usual sense. Rows arrive via a different mechanism.' Explicitly contrasts populate (upstream sources) with insert (master + part).
